title: Should you keep speaking the language you're worse at?
summary: Argue whether two friends who share two languages should keep struggling in the weaker one to improve, or switch to the fluent one and protect the friendship from the friction of bad grammar.
topic: The tradeoff between using a friendship to practise a language and keeping the friendship easy by speaking the language you both master
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay on whether two friends who share both a fluent common language and a weaker one should deliberately keep speaking the weaker language to improve, or default to the fluent one to keep the friendship effortless. Take a clear position on this tradeoff between growth and ease. Use two concrete examples and concede one fair objection. Include at least one cleft sentence, one passive construction, and concession language such as 'admittedly' or 'true'.
hintStarters: When two friends share two languages, the one they choose says more about what they want from each other than…
What you lose by switching to the easy language is not just practice but a particular version of your friend, the one who…
Admittedly, a friendship is not a free language class, and few people sign up to have their grammar corrected by someone they…
Speaking badly in front of someone you trust is a small humiliation, and it is precisely that humiliation that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tự động chuyển sang / dùng theo mặc định
ngượng ngùng / để ý ánh nhìn
sự ăn ý / mối quan hệ tốt
đơn giản hóa / làm hời hợt đi

sampleText:
When two friends share two languages, the one they choose says more about what they want from each other than they usually admit. My friend and I can speak in his language, where I am clumsy, or in mine, where he is. For years we drifted, without deciding, into mine — and only later did I notice what that quiet default had cost. My position is that the friendship should hold the line in the weaker language, not despite the friction but because of it, provided both people actually agree to it.

The case for switching to the fluent language is obvious and seductive: ease. Conversation moves, jokes land, nuance survives. But what you lose by switching is not just practice but a particular version of your friend — the one who is patient, who slows down, who lets you be inarticulate without scoring it. A friendship conducted only in the language you both master never has to make room for the other person's struggle, and a friendship that has never made room for struggle is shallower than it knows. It is in the weaker language that you find out whether your friend will wait for you.

Consider two students, one Korean and one Brazilian, who met in an English-speaking city. They could have lived their whole friendship in fluent English and many such pairs do, parking their own tongues at the door. Instead they agreed to spend half their time in Korean and half in Portuguese, badly. The conversations were slower and dumber. They were also more honest, because a person speaking a weaker language cannot hide behind cleverness; the showing-off is stripped away, and what is left is closer to who they actually are. Each was seen, in the other's language, with the polish removed.

Contrast that with a couple I know who always switched to the wife's fluent language 'just for now', for a decade. He understood her world; she never quite entered his. When his mother visited and spoke no English, he realised his closest person could not meet his family in their own words. The easy choice had, year by year, built a wall exactly where it felt like a convenience.

Admittedly, a friendship is not a free language class, and few people sign up to have their grammar corrected by someone they wanted to relax with. True — and that objection sets a real limit. Correction without consent is a small act of condescension, and a relationship turned into a permanent lesson stops being a relationship. There are also evenings when you are tired, or grieving, or simply need to be understood quickly, and reaching for the easy language is then not laziness but kindness. The weaker language should be a shared choice, never a tax one friend levies on the other.

Even so, the deeper objection answers itself. Speaking badly in front of someone you trust is a small humiliation, and it is precisely that humiliation that the fluent language lets you avoid — which is exactly why avoiding it costs something. The friendships that grow are usually the ones willing to be a little awkward on purpose. To always choose the easy language is to choose the easy friendship, and the easy friendship, like the easy conversation, is the one that asks the least of you and, in the end, gives back about as much.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The case for switching to the fluent language is obvious and seductive: ease
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Lý lẽ cho việc chuyển sang ngôn ngữ lưu loát thì rõ ràng và quyến rũ: sự dễ dàng

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences to relocate what is really at stake
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is in the weaker language that you find out whether your friend will wait for you' (it-cleft) và 'it is precisely that humiliation that the fluent language lets you avoid' (it-cleft) dùng câu chẻ để dời trọng tâm sang điều thật sự bị đánh đổi: phép thử về sự kiên nhẫn và cái giá của việc né tránh sự bẽ mặt.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is in the weaker language that you find out whether your friend will wait for you' (it-cleft) and 'it is precisely that humiliation that the fluent language lets you avoid' (it-cleft) use clefts to relocate the real stakes: the test of patience and the cost of avoiding embarrassment.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to depersonalise honesty and exposure
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the showing-off is stripped away', 'Each was seen … with the polish removed', và 'a tax one friend levies on the other' (chủ động đối lập) cho thấy bị động được dùng để khắc họa sự phơi bày như điều xảy đến với người nói, làm nổi bật trạng thái dễ tổn thương khi dùng ngôn ngữ yếu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'the showing-off is stripped away' and 'Each was seen … with the polish removed' use the passive to portray exposure as something that happens to the speaker, foregrounding the vulnerability of using the weaker language.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi hai người bạn cùng chia sẻ hai ngôn ngữ, ngôn ngữ họ chọn nói lên về điều họ muốn từ nhau nhiều hơn họ thường thừa nhận. Bạn tôi và tôi có thể nói bằng ngôn ngữ của cậu ấy, nơi tôi vụng về, hoặc bằng ngôn ngữ của tôi, nơi cậu ấy vụng về. Suốt nhiều năm chúng tôi trôi dạt, không quyết định, vào ngôn ngữ của tôi — và mãi về sau tôi mới nhận ra cái mặc định lặng lẽ ấy đã trả giá những gì. Lập trường của tôi là tình bạn nên giữ vững lập trường trong ngôn ngữ yếu hơn, không phải bất chấp sự cọ xát mà chính vì nó, miễn là cả hai người thực sự đồng ý điều đó.

Lý lẽ cho việc chuyển sang ngôn ngữ lưu loát thì rõ ràng và quyến rũ: sự dễ dàng. Cuộc trò chuyện trôi chảy, câu đùa có duyên, sắc thái sống sót. Nhưng cái bạn mất khi chuyển ngôn ngữ không chỉ là luyện tập mà là một phiên bản đặc biệt của người bạn — phiên bản kiên nhẫn, chịu chậm lại, người để bạn ấp úng không trôi chảy mà không tính điểm bạn. Một tình bạn chỉ diễn ra trong ngôn ngữ mà cả hai đều thành thạo thì chẳng bao giờ phải nhường chỗ cho sự vật lộn của người kia, và một tình bạn chưa bao giờ nhường chỗ cho sự vật lộn thì nông cạn hơn nó tưởng. Chính trong ngôn ngữ yếu hơn mà bạn phát hiện liệu người bạn của mình có chờ bạn hay không.

Hãy xét hai sinh viên, một người Hàn và một người Brazil, gặp nhau ở một thành phố nói tiếng Anh. Họ lẽ ra đã có thể sống trọn tình bạn của mình bằng tiếng Anh lưu loát và nhiều cặp như thế đã làm vậy, gửi lại tiếng mẹ đẻ của họ ở ngoài cửa. Thay vào đó họ thỏa thuận dành nửa thời gian cho tiếng Hàn và nửa cho tiếng Bồ Đào Nha, một cách dở tệ. Những cuộc trò chuyện chậm hơn và ngốc nghếch hơn. Chúng cũng thành thật hơn, bởi một người nói ngôn ngữ yếu hơn không thể núp sau sự lanh lợi; sự khoe mẽ bị lột bỏ, và cái còn lại gần với con người thật của họ hơn. Mỗi người được nhìn thấy, trong ngôn ngữ của người kia, với lớp đánh bóng đã được gỡ đi.

Hãy đối chiếu điều đó với một cặp vợ chồng tôi quen, những người luôn chuyển sang ngôn ngữ lưu loát của người vợ 'chỉ tạm thời thôi', suốt một thập kỷ. Anh hiểu thế giới của cô; cô chưa bao giờ thật sự bước vào thế giới của anh. Khi mẹ anh đến thăm và không nói được tiếng Anh, anh nhận ra người gần gũi nhất của mình không thể gặp gia đình anh bằng chính ngôn ngữ của họ. Lựa chọn dễ dàng đã, năm này qua năm khác, dựng lên một bức tường ngay tại chỗ mà nó cảm thấy như một sự tiện lợi.

Công nhận, một tình bạn không phải một lớp học ngôn ngữ miễn phí, và ít người đăng ký để bị sửa ngữ pháp bởi một người mà họ muốn được thư giãn cùng. Đúng vậy — và phản đối đó đặt ra một giới hạn có thật. Sửa lỗi mà không có sự đồng ý là một hành vi trịch thượng nho nhỏ, và một mối quan hệ bị biến thành một bài học vĩnh viễn thì thôi không còn là một mối quan hệ. Cũng có những buổi tối bạn mệt mỏi, hay đang đau buồn, hay đơn giản cần được hiểu nhanh chóng, và với tay đến ngôn ngữ dễ khi ấy không phải là lười biếng mà là sự tử tế. Ngôn ngữ yếu hơn nên là một lựa chọn chung, không bao giờ là một khoản thuế người bạn này áp lên người kia.

Dẫu vậy, phản đối sâu xa hơn tự nó trả lời chính nó. Nói dở trước mặt người bạn tin tưởng là một sự bẽ mặt nho nhỏ, và chính sự bẽ mặt ấy là điều mà ngôn ngữ lưu loát cho phép bạn tránh được — đó chính xác là lý do việc né tránh nó phải trả một cái giá. Những tình bạn lớn lên thường là những tình bạn sẵn lòng hơi vụng về một cách có chủ ý. Luôn chọn ngôn ngữ dễ là chọn tình bạn dễ, và tình bạn dễ dàng, như cuộc trò chuyện dễ dàng, là thứ đòi hỏi ở bạn ít nhất và, rốt cuộc, trả lại cũng chừng ấy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to locate where the test of friendship happens?
options: You find out in the weaker language whether your friend will wait which is for you.
In the weaker language find out it is you whether wait will your friend.
It is in the weaker language that you find out whether your friend will wait for you.
Whether your friend will wait it is you find out the weaker language in that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is in X that …' is an it-cleft spotlighting the weaker language as where the test occurs.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is in the weaker language that …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to name what the fluent language lets you dodge?
options: The fluent language lets you avoid that humiliation which is precisely it.
That humiliation it is the fluent language precisely lets avoid you which.
Avoid you lets the fluent language it is that humiliation precisely which.
It is precisely that humiliation that the fluent language lets you avoid.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is precisely X that …' is an it-cleft foregrounding the humiliation being avoided.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is precisely that humiliation that …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to depersonalise the speaker's exposure?
options: The showing-off is stripped away, and what is left is closer to who they actually are.
They strip away the showing-off so each person sees the other with polish.
Stripping away the showing-off is what each person seeing the other does.
Away is stripped the showing-off and closer what left is who they are.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Is stripped away' is passive, framing the exposure as something that happens to the speaker, not an act by a doer.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is + past participle' to hide who removes the showing-off?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to default to' mean as the essay uses it?
options: To fail to pay a debt
To fall back on something automatically without choosing it deliberately
To refuse to use something
To translate something carefully
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Default to' here means slipping into the fluent language by habit rather than choice.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of the option a system falls back on when no choice is made.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option concedes the objection fairly and in the essay's register?
options: People who won't practise are simply too lazy to deserve a bilingual friend.
Obviously you must always speak the harder language or the friendship is fake.
Admittedly, a friendship is not a free language class, and few people sign up to have their grammar corrected by someone they wanted to relax with.
Of course correcting your friends constantly is the only real way to show you care.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly, …' genuinely grants that friendship is not a lesson before the essay answers the point.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option respects the friend who just wants to relax?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges or qualifies its claim appropriately?
options: Reaching for the easy language is always pure laziness with no exceptions.
Reaching for the easy language never helps anyone in any situation.
Reaching for laziness the easy language is then but kindness not when evenings.
There are evenings when you are tired or grieving, and reaching for the easy language is then not laziness but kindness.
mcQuestions.explanation: The option qualifies the claim to specific evenings, avoiding the absolutes 'always' and 'never'.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'always' and 'never'; pick the option that allows exceptions.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the writer's stated position on the tradeoff?
options: The friendship should hold the line in the weaker language, because of the friction, provided both agree
Always switch to the fluent language to protect the friendship
Friends should never share more than one language
The weaker language should be forced on the more fluent friend
mcQuestions.explanation: The thesis favours the weaker language 'because of the friction', but only by mutual agreement.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the final sentence of the first paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: Honestly just speak whatever, who even cares about grammar that much, lol.
A friendship that has never made room for struggle is shallower than it knows.
Basically the easy language is kinda fake and the hard one is more real, you know?
It's super annoying being corrected, so just don't bother, simple as that.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the claim precisely; the others slide into casual filler.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best moves from the concession back to the argument?
options: In conclusion, language never matters in friendship.
Anyway, none of this is worth thinking about.
Even so, the deeper objection answers itself: the humiliation the fluent language avoids is exactly why avoiding it costs something.
On the contrary, correcting friends is always the kindest thing to do.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession just made and turns it back toward the thesis.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase honours the concession yet reasserts the position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to plateau' mean as a learner's experience?
options: To improve rapidly all at once
To forget a language entirely
To travel to a high flat place
To stop making progress and stay at the same level
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Plateau' means progress flattening out so the learner stops improving.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a graph that stops rising and goes flat.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most cleanly?
options: Conversation moves, jokes land, nuance survives
Conversation moves and the jokes are landing and to survive nuance
Moving conversation, the jokes landed, and nuance to survive
Conversation the moves, land jokes, surviving nuance does
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched short clauses ('X verbs') form a clean parallel triad; the others break the pattern.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three clauses in the same simple shape?

mcQuestions.prompt: What did the easy choice cost the couple in the essay's example?
options: They both forgot how to speak any language at all
The wife never learned the husband's language and could not meet his family in their own words
They were forced to move to a new country
The husband refused to ever speak English again
mcQuestions.explanation: Defaulting to her fluent language 'just for now' for a decade walled off his family from her.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the paragraph about the mother's visit.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'condescension' mean as used here?
options: A generous compliment
A formal apology
A patronising attitude that treats someone as inferior
A careful translation
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Condescension' is looking down on someone, as uninvited correction can feel.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of being talked down to.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: The easy language is always the wrong choice in every situation.
Friendships should be turned into permanent language lessons.
Only fluent conversation can ever build a real friendship.
Always choosing the easy language is choosing the easy friendship — the one that asks the least and gives back about as much.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion links the easy language to the easy, shallow friendship without forbidding the easy choice outright.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option tying ease of language to ease of friendship.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the em-dash insertion correctly to add a defining aside?
options: What you lose by switching is not just practice but a particular version of your friend — the one who is patient, who slows down, who lets you be inarticulate.
What you lose — by switching is not just practice but — a version of your friend the one patient.
A particular version — what you lose by switching — of your friend not just practice who is patient.
Not just practice — what you lose by switching is — a version patient who slows the friend.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dash introduces a clarifying description of the friend; the main clause stands alone without it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed part and keep a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'effortless' mean as used here?
options: Requiring great skill to achieve
Done easily, without strain or difficulty
Pointless or wasteful
Done by force
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Effortless' describes the smooth ease of the fluent language, the comfort the essay weighs against growth.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something that takes no struggle at all.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a relationship where you and the other person share two languages unequally. Which one do you fall into by default — and if you switched to the one you're worse at for a month, what do you think you would learn about the other person that the easy language has been hiding?
